quote: Yeah. It shows how outdated Google Maps is. Same. That's crazy. Sweet. Well, I'm glad that worked out there for you.
stewart_read: David's storm story is a genuine rapport moment — he's animated, sharing something personal, and the rep matches his energy naturally ('That's crazy,' 'I'm glad that worked out'). This is the rep at his best: calm, human, not rushing past the moment. The exchange lands as neighbor-like rather than salesperson-like.
coaching_implication: Reinforce this in the 1-on-1. The rep has the energy-match instinct — the coaching job is to show him how to carry that same naturalness into the structural beats (bill anchor, close) rather than letting it live only in the rapport window.

quote: my son recently purchased a house in North Portland, and his house has got huge numbers of solar panels... his bill is very small. So why not?
stewart_read: David is providing pre-built social proof from a trusted source (his son) and self-motivating with 'why not?' — this is the call's highest-value qualification signal. The rep acknowledged it ('There you go. Yeah.') but did not anchor on it, deepen it, or tie it back later in the call. A line like 'Your son's already seen what this looks like — let's get you the same picture for your home' would have extended the energy peak into the bill collection and close.
coaching_implication: Show the rep this moment in the 1-on-1. Ask: 'What did David just hand you here, and what did you do with it?' The son reference is a re-engagement anchor the closer can also use — it belongs in the handoff brief.

quote: I honestly don't know. I honestly don't know. Someone else asked me to ask the question and I it's an honor to deduct, you know, so I because, you know, out of sight, out of mind.
stewart_read: David says he doesn't know his average PGE bill and the rep responds 'No. I I understand' and moves on. This is the codex's canonical bill_anchor failure pattern: ask + acknowledge + move on. The bill was not captured, not flipped, and not bridged to the gas-primary concern David had already raised. The rep had an opening — 'out of sight, out of mind' is itself an emotional signal about disengagement from utility costs — and walked past it.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep may have planned to recover the bill via the email exchange. Rejected — the email path was David's suggestion, not the rep's plan, and the rep made no attempt to anchor the bill emotionally before pivoting to email.
coaching_implication: This is the floor's universal gap per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring. In the 1-on-1: 'When David said out of sight, out of mind, what was he telling you about how he feels about his bill?' Then walk through the capture-and-flip sequence. The flip doesn't require knowing the number — the emotion of not knowing is itself leverage.

quote: it's actually gas is the primary in the house.
stewart_read: David flags gas-primary as his first pass at the concern — the rep says 'Oh, gotcha' and moves on. This is the first touch of a two-touch pattern. The rep had an opportunity to acknowledge the gas situation and bridge to the electric portion solar addresses, but instead treated it as a neutral data point. The second touch (08:13) becomes a direct challenge precisely because the first touch was unresolved.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep may have planned to address gas-vs-electric at the design stage. Rejected — the second touch at 08:13 shows David still unresolved, and the rep's response there is also flat. No evidence of a planned later move.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1: 'David told you gas was primary at 03:16. What did you do with that?' Walk the rep through a bridge: acknowledging the gas situation, then pivoting to the electric portion solar addresses, then using the son's low bill as proof the math still works. This is a two-touch miss — the first pass set up the second.

quote: for me, energy usage is the gas furnace that's on at 70 degrees all the time. I don't mean to be accusatory, but you understand what I'm saying as far as I'm concerned, and that's the energy usage. So you're saying it's only electric. Is that right?
stewart_read: Second touch of the gas-primary pattern. David is now direct and slightly apologetic — 'I don't mean to be accusatory' signals he's pulling back emotionally. The rep confirms solar only addresses electric and pivots to bill collection without bridging to residual value. The correct move here is to acknowledge the limitation honestly, then bridge: 'You're right — solar doesn't touch the gas bill. But your electric bill is still real, and your son's house proves the math works even when gas is primary.' Instead, the rep confirms the limitation and moves on, leaving David's concern unresolved.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the 03:16 entry in the 1-on-1 — show the rep the two-touch pattern. The second touch is harder to recover because the first was missed. The coaching goal is to catch the first touch early so the second never becomes a challenge.

quote: So I have you here on the title of the home, David. Is there anyone else on the title with you at all? My wife.
stewart_read: The rep confirms the wife is on title and says 'Okay. Sounds good. Awesome.' — and never returns to spouse inclusion for the appointment. When the close attempt comes at 09:21, it's directed at David alone. The wife is on title, has a stroke-related homebound status (making her schedule a real constraint), and is never anchored into the appointment structure. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct move is to include the spouse in the appointment at the moment of title confirmation.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep may have planned to include the wife at the close. Rejected — the close at 09:21 makes no mention of the wife, and the soft callback at 09:55 also omits her.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1: 'You confirmed the wife is on title at 04:07. Where did she go after that?' Walk through the spouse-inclusion protocol. Note that the wife's homebound status (raised at 03:13) actually makes her easier to include — she's home. The rep had a natural bridge and didn't use it.

quote: we would just need to find a good time for we actually do have a specialist in your area here for the next couple days. Could just find a good time for them to stop by.
stewart_read: The rep attempts an in-person visit without first offering a phone appointment — the codex's default format. David declines citing schedule and wife's condition. The rep then retreats to an unanchored callback with no date, no time block, no 'is that fair' handshake, and no phone-appointment alternative offered. This is a single-angle close attempt followed by full retreat, missing button_up required elements: appointment_datetime_confirmed, one_hour_block_stated, phone_appointment_specified, is_that_fair_handshake.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: rep accepted the deferral to preserve goodwill and planned to re-engage via email. Rejected — there is no evidence of a planned re-engagement structure, and the email path was David's suggestion, not the rep's.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1: 'When David said it's not gonna happen this week, what were your options?' Walk through the phone-appointment alternative — David is home with his wife, a 30-minute phone call is lower friction than an in-person visit. Then walk through the button_up checklist. The goal is to show the rep that 'no to in-person' is not 'no to everything.'
